intro: Your verdict is sealed. Now the other eleven jurors take the same evidence into the room. Listen.

jurors.label: Juror 2
jurors.persona: Takes the numbers at face value — four accusers cannot all be wrong.

jurors.label: Juror 3
jurors.persona: Practical; trusts what was found and what was written.

jurors.label: Juror 4
jurors.persona: The principled holdout; fixes on how brief and how old each sighting was.

jurors.label: Juror 5
jurors.persona: Keeps the room honest about the burden of proof.

jurors.label: Juror 6
jurors.persona: Foreperson; keeps order and leans to convict.

jurors.label: Juror 8
jurors.persona: Comes in certain; undone by the parade.

jurors.label: Juror 9
jurors.persona: Quiet doubter; distrusts certainty offered too easily.

jurors.label: Juror 10
jurors.persona: Wants the accused to account for himself.

jurors.label: Juror 11
jurors.persona: The undecided one; talks himself into a verdict.

jurors.label: Juror 12
jurors.persona: Uneasy about the identifications, swayed by their number.

script.speaker: J-05
script.text: Juror One has sealed a verdict; the rest of us must reach our own. Round the table, then — guilty or not, and the reason that weighs most.

script.speaker: J-01
script.text: Four women, none of them acquainted, and every one names the same man. I cannot make that a coincidence. Guilty.

script.speaker: J-03
script.text: Each saw a stranger for a few minutes — in a doorway, on a step — and picked him out months afterward, once the papers had run his description. That is not memory. That is suggestion.

script.speaker: J-09
script.text: If he is the wrong man, let him tell us where he was on each of those days. He cannot.

script.speaker: J-04
script.text: That is not his burden. He need prove nothing. It is for the Crown to prove it was him, and to prove it so that we are sure.

script.speaker: J-06
script.text: The handwriting man was certain. The lists were in the accused's hand, disguised.

script.speaker: J-08
script.text: He also granted the hand was disguised. A disguise one man can put on, another can put on for him. Certainty like that makes me less sure, not more.

script.speaker: J-02
script.text: And the good clothes — the spats — found in his room, matching how the caller was dressed.

script.speaker: J-03
script.text: Fine dress is not a crime. Half the gentlemen in town are turned out the same.

script.speaker: J-07
script.text: I came in sure. But I keep returning to the parade — they were shown him one at a time, the first woman hesitated, and the rest followed where she led. If she was wrong, they were all wrong together. I cannot call that being sure.

script.speaker: J-10
script.text: I have gone back and forth all afternoon. But four accounts, the writing, the matter from years before — set side by side, it is too much to lay to chance. I say guilty.

script.speaker: J-11
script.text: I do not trust the identifications either. Yet I cannot get past how many of them there are. Guilty, then.

script.speaker: J-03
script.text: Then I will be the one who cannot be sure. That is what the doubt is for.

script.speaker: J-05
script.text: We are not of one mind. But we have enough to return. Let it be counted.
